attributes.stem:
A nine-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parent with a three-week history of an intensely itchy rash. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. His immunisations are up to date. Only the boy has symptoms in the house, and he denies any similar previous symptoms. They have just moved to community housing where there are many stray animals in the area.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The rash consists of symmetrically distributed, erythematous papules and vesicles on his elbows, knees, and buttocks. Some areas show excoriation due to scratching. The parent mentions that the boy has been complaining of occasional abdominal discomfort after meals.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation in this case is highly suggestive of dermatitis herpetiformis (DH). The characteristic features include intensely pruritic papulovesicular lesions symmetrically distributed on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees) and buttocks. The mention of abdominal discomfort is also relevant, as DH is strongly associated with celiac disease and gluten sensitivity.

The most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis is a skin biopsy for direct immunofluorescence. This test is considered the gold standard for diagnosing dermatitis herpetiformis. It typically shows granular deposits of IgA in the dermal papillae. This finding is pathognomonic for DH and is present even in uninvolved skin.

A Burrow ink test is used to detect a 'zig zag' line from scabies infestation. Whilst the intense itch and scratch marks, nodules and vesicles is present with scabies, as well as being associated with overcrowded areas, scabies is not associated with abdominal discomfort. It is also more likely that the parents would have similar symptoms if this was a scabies infection as it is rather contagious.

A skin prick test is used to diagnose IgE-mediated allergies by introducing small amounts of allergens into the skin and observing for an immediate reaction. It is not useful for diagnosing dermatitis herpetiformis, which is not an IgE-mediated condition.

A patch test is used to diagnose allergic contact dermatitis by applying potential allergens to the skin for 48 hours and observing for a delayed hypersensitivity reaction. This test is not appropriate for diagnosing dermatitis herpetiformis.

Measuring serum IgE levels is helpful in diagnosing and monitoring allergic conditions such as atopic dermatitis or allergic asthma. Dermatitis is not often intensely itchy as well. Dermatitis herpetiformis is not associated with elevated IgE levels, so this test would not be useful in confirming the diagnosis.

A Tzanck smear is a cytological test primarily used to diagnose herpes simplex or varicella zoster virus infections. It involves scraping the base of a vesicle and examining the cells under a microscope. This test is not useful for diagnosing dermatitis herpetiformis.

After confirming the diagnosis of dermatitis herpetiformis, further investigation for coeliac disease (including serological tests and possibly small bowel biopsy) would be warranted. The family should also be evaluated for possible coeliac disease. Treatment is with a life-long gluten-free diet which demonstrates resolution of the rash in weeks to months (and up to two years), and may also include dapsone for symptomatic relief.
learningPoints: Dermatitis herpetiformis is strongly associated with celiac disease and gluten sensitivity.
Direct immunofluorescence showing granular IgA deposits in dermal papillae is diagnostic for dermatitis herpetiformis.
references: Maguire J, Mitchel G. Scabies test. Dermnet NZ. 2022.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/scabies-test
Al-Tameemi, A, Elyoussifi S, Coulson I. Dermatitis herpetiformis. Dermnet NZ. 2023. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/dermatitis-herpetiformis

Reunala T, Hervonen K, Salmi T. Dermatitis Herpetiformis: An Update on Diagnosis and Management. Am J Clin Dermatol. 2021 May;22(3):329-338.  Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8068693/
